items.myth: Curse of dimensionality means you should never use more than a few features.
items.reality: The curse describes geometry and sample needs as d grows — not a hard cap. High d is workable when n is large enough, features carry signal, intrinsic dimension is low, or the model does not rely on dense local neighborhoods.
items.example: Sparse text with 50k word dimensions can work with linear SVMs because the model is global and sparse, not k-NN over dense local balls.

items.myth: Distance concentration makes all distance-based methods useless in any d > 10.
items.reality: Concentration severity depends on distribution, scaling, and correlation. Moderate d with informative scaled features still supports useful k-NN; the effect is gradual, not a cliff at d = 10.
items.example: Iris with d = 4 scaled features: k-NN remains a strong baseline — far below regimes where concentration dominates.

items.myth: The curse only affects k-NN, not neural networks or trees.
items.reality: Any method that implicitly needs local coverage or exhaustive grids suffers sample-complexity blowup; trees partition axis-aligned but still split in high d with limited n; NNs can learn low-dimensional structure but need data or inductive bias when ambient d is huge.
items.example: Deep tabular net on 5k rows × 2k noise columns overfits like k-NN — different mechanism, same insufficient local/global evidence.

items.myth: Collecting 10× more rows always fixes high-dimensional sparsity.
items.reality: Required n often scales exponentially with d for fixed resolution; 10× more data may be negligible when d increases by 20 irrelevant columns.
items.example: n: 1k → 10k helps in d = 5; same 10× gain barely changes neighbor purity when d jumps to 500 with random features.
